investmentHypotheses: Correspondent banking's multi-day settlement delay is a structural inefficiency banks will pay to remove, not a fixed cost of the system.
A netting layer that plugs into existing core banking systems can win without requiring banks to change custody providers.

strengths: Founder built the exact system category at a top-5 card network
Two bank LOIs already in hand
weaknesses: Sales cycle to banks historically 9-18 months
Single technical founder
opportunities: Regulatory push toward real-time settlement in several markets
Expansion into treasury management adjacent product
threats: Card networks could build competing internal tooling
Correspondent banks have switching-cost inertia

problemProduct.problem: Cross-border settlement between mid-size banks still takes 2-4 business days through correspondent banking chains, tying up working capital and creating FX exposure.
problemProduct.product: A real-time netting engine that plugs into a bank's core system via API and settles obligations against a shared ledger every 15 minutes instead of daily batch cycles.

teamHistory.value: Founding team: Priya Chandrasekaran. Solo technical founder with a directly relevant acquisition exit in the same problem space; actively recruiting a business co-founder with bank BD experience.

technologyDefensibility.value: Netting engine built on an event-sourced ledger with idempotent settlement batches; architecture reviewed favorably by an independent payments infrastructure engineer.

value.note: SAM defined as mid-size regional banks in NA/EU currently reliant on correspondent banking for cross-border settlement.

value.name: Wire-based fintech rails
value.positioning: Faster than correspondent banking but not real-time netting

exitPerspective.value: The most likely path is a strategic acquisition by a card network (e.g. Visa or Mastercard) or a payments infrastructure incumbent looking to buy real-time settlement capability rather than build it in-house — comparable payments-infrastructure acquisitions have closed at 4-7x ARR. A slower-growth scenario could see a private-equity roll-up fold this into a broader payments-modernization platform alongside similar infrastructure assets. Either buyer would likely pay a premium because replicating bank-grade settlement rails from scratch typically takes two to three years and carries regulatory risk an established player would rather acquire than build.

decision.rationale: Founder-market fit is as strong as this fund sees; the product is a clear wedge into a real structural cost banks want removed, and two LOIs de-risk the initial GTM.

adversarialView.counterThesis: Enterprise sales cycles to banks routinely blow past founder projections, and a solo technical founder without a BD co-founder may stall exactly where this deal needs speed.
keyRisks: Key-person risk on a solo founder
Regulatory approval timeline for settlement products varies by market
adversarialView.whatWouldChangeOurMind: A third LOI converting to a signed paid pilot within 60 days would remove our biggest concern about GTM speed.

portfolioFit.diversificationNote: Adds fintech infrastructure exposure; portfolio is currently light on regulated-adjacent B2B infra.

investmentHypotheses: Carbon credit buyers will pay a premium for satellite-verified MRV over manual sampling as compliance markets mature.
A licensable measurement methodology can become the default verification layer across multiple credit registries.

strengths: Peer-reviewed methodology co-founder authored
Early registry partnership in progress
weaknesses: Sales cycle depends on registry approval timelines outside company control
Revenue concentrated in one geography
opportunities: Compliance carbon markets expanding faster than voluntary markets
Adjacent application to deforestation monitoring
threats: Larger remote-sensing incumbents could add soil carbon as a feature
Registry approval could stall indefinitely

problemProduct.problem: Soil carbon credit issuance relies on manual, expensive sampling that limits how much land can realistically be verified and creates audit risk for buyers.
problemProduct.product: A satellite-imagery pipeline that estimates soil organic carbon at parcel-level resolution and outputs registry-ready MRV reports.

technologyDefensibility.value: Computer vision pipeline trained on ground-truth soil samples collected across three growing seasons; independent accuracy validated against manual sampling within 8% margin.

value.note: SAM limited to compliance-market MRV spend in geographies with active soil carbon credit programs.

decision.rationale: Strong technical differentiation and founder-market fit, but registry approval — the single gating dependency for revenue — is outside the company's control and timeline is uncertain.

adversarialView.counterThesis: If registry approval slips another 2 quarters, the company may run out of runway before revenue starts, regardless of product quality.
keyRisks: Prior venture from lead founder wound down for funding reasons, not product reasons — but pattern worth noting
adversarialView.whatWouldChangeOurMind: Confirmed registry approval date would move this to invest.

portfolioFit.diversificationNote: Would add climate/carbon exposure; portfolio currently has none.

investmentHypotheses: Copy-on-write database branching removes a real bottleneck in CI pipelines that currently rely on stale fixtures or full clones.
Open-source-led adoption among individual engineers can convert into team-level paid contracts faster than top-down enterprise sales.

strengths: 4,000+ GitHub stars organically, no marketing spend
Deep technical credibility from founder's OSS contributions
weaknesses: No enterprise sales experience on the team
Free tier usage far exceeds paid conversions so far
opportunities: Expansion beyond CI into staging-environment branching
Natural fit for usage-based pricing at scale
threats: Managed Postgres vendors could ship native branching
Category awareness still early; may require significant education spend

problemProduct.problem: CI pipelines either run against stale fixture data or pay the cost (in time and infra) of cloning full production-like databases for every test run.
problemProduct.product: A CLI and API that create instant, isolated, copy-on-write Postgres branches scoped to a single CI run or pull request.

teamHistory.value: Founding team: Dev Patel. Single first-time founder, highly credible technically; deck flags an open technical co-founder search but no hire made yet.

technologyDefensibility.value: Built on Postgres WAL-based copy-on-write branching; independently reviewed as sound by an outside database engineer, though scaling beyond single-region deployments is unproven.

value.name: Managed Postgres providers (branching features)
value.positioning: Adjacent incumbents who could bundle a competing feature

decision.rationale: Best product-led growth signal we've seen this quarter pre-revenue; the technical wedge is real and the founder has unusually deep credibility in exactly this niche.

adversarialView.counterThesis: Free-to-paid conversion is currently near zero, and the single-founder team has no proven enterprise sales muscle to fix that.
keyRisks: Solo founder, no business co-founder yet
Platform risk if a managed Postgres vendor ships a native competitor
adversarialView.whatWouldChangeOurMind: First 10 paid team-tier conversions would substantially de-risk monetization concerns.

portfolioFit.diversificationNote: Adds developer tooling exposure alongside existing infra bets.
